sound_type: shrill dissonant flock call -- high-pitched nasal bark 'honk' or 'whouk' at 600-4000 Hz, individually harsh and unmusical, but amplified by flock of 10,000-50,000+ into continuous dissonant roar, E=5 flock-scale overwhelm, mass liftoffs physically immersive
ecosystem: Delta, winter visitor. Skagit Valley agricultural fields and tidal flats, Fir Island. Breeds on Arctic tundra. Massive winter flocks of tens of thousands.
season: Winter visitor (October-March). Breeds on Arctic tundra in summer. Peak numbers December through February.

coordinates.description: Reference for Fir Island, Skagit Valley -- primary Snow Goose concentration area. Tens of thousands of birds in agricultural fields.

taxonomy_note.description: SIXTEENTH consecutive Anatidae (118-133). Second Anser genus entry (after Greater White-fronted Goose, 132). Some authorities place Snow Goose in genus Chen. E=5 reflects the sheer overwhelming volume of Snow Goose flocks. Fifth goose in the goose era (129-134). White and blue morphs -- single-gene polymorphism. Population explosion (15M+) destroying Arctic breeding habitat. Conservation irony.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Anatidae (16th consecutive -- streak ongoing, one more entry)
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae: sixteen consecutive at degrees 118-133. Nearly doubled the Picidae record (9).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Anser from Latin 'goose.' caerulescens from Latin 'becoming blue' -- referring to the blue morph, which was described and named first. The species was named from the dark form, not the white.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Anser -- second entry (after Greater White-fronted Goose, 132). Some authorities use Chen for Snow Goose. AOS currently treats it as Anser caerulescens.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae at sixteenth consecutive. One more entry (Ross's Goose, 134) before the streak ends.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 600-4,000 Hz. Highest-pitched goose in the engine. The shrill quality comes from energy concentrated in the 1,500-3,500 Hz range.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,500-3,500 Hz. Higher and shriller than all other PNW geese. Piercing quality to the human ear.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Flock call: 600-4,000 Hz. Individual bark: one syllable. Alarm: sharper, more rapid.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual calls 0.3-1.0 seconds. One syllable. Given in rapid series when alarmed.
song_metrics.note_structure: SHRILL NASAL BARK: One-syllable 'honk' or 'whouk' or yelping 'awk.' High-pitched. Relatively simple waveform. Harsh and unmusical individually. Collectively overwhelming.
song_metrics.note_types: Flock contact call (primary), alarm call (sharper, more rapid), flight call (continuous chattering), feeding murmur (softer).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Continuous overlapping calls in large flocks. No structured phrase -- the flock produces a broadband noise field from thousands of simultaneous vocalizations.
song_metrics.timbre: Shrill. Nasal. Harsh. Piercing. Lacks the musical quality of Greater White-fronted Goose (132) or the resonant depth of Canada Goose (129).
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Individual call is moderate. Flock of 10,000-50,000+ produces overwhelming volume. Mass liftoffs: physically immersive. Audible for a mile or more.
song_metrics.tempo: Rapid and continuous in flocks. Individual calls at 1-3 second intervals. In alarm, near-continuous.
song_metrics.context: Flock communication, flight coordination, alarm, contact. The flock sound is constant when birds are active. Mass liftoffs produce acoustic climax events.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Pacific flyway birds (Wrangel Island breeding population) vs. mid-continent birds. Minimal vocal variation. Blue morph frequency varies geographically.
song_metrics.key_property: SHRILL DISSONANT FLOCK CALL -- E=5 FLOCK-SCALE OVERWHELM. Nasal bark at 600-4,000 Hz. Tens of thousands of birds producing continuous wall of dissonant sound. Mass liftoffs physically immersive. SIXTEENTH CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE (118-133). White and blue morphs -- single-gene polymorphism. Conservation irony: population explosion destroying Arctic habitat. 15M+ birds. Skagit Valley spectacle. 'White blizzard.'

plumage.overall: White morph: entirely white with black wingtips. Pink bill with black 'grinning patch.' Pink legs. Blue morph: dark gray to blue-gray body with white head and upper neck. Both morphs: pink bill and legs.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: White morph: all-white with black wingtips. Blue morph: dark body with white head. Pink bill with black grinning patch in both morphs.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Juveniles: grayish overall (white morph juveniles), darker gray (blue morph juveniles). Adults: clean plumage. Staining: rusty facial staining common from feeding in iron-rich soils.
plumage.flight_pattern: Massive flocks in V-formation or swirling masses. White birds against gray winter sky = 'white blizzard.' Black wingtips visible. The visual spectacle matches the acoustic overwhelm.

physical_description.overall: Body length 64-79 cm (25-31 inches). Weight 2.0-3.5 kg (4.4-7.7 lbs). Wingspan 132-165 cm. Medium-large goose. Similar size to Greater White-fronted Goose but chunkier.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: White morph unmistakable: all-white goose with pink bill and black wingtips. Blue morph: dark body, white head, pink bill. Grinning patch (black line on bill edge) diagnostic in both morphs.
physical_description.field_id: In the Skagit Valley: look for white blankets in agricultural fields visible from a distance. Listen for the continuous shrill hum. At closer range: pink bills with black grinning patch, black wingtips. Scan for blue morphs among the white mass.

nodes.title: Shrill Dissonant Wall of Sound -- E=5 Flock-Scale Overwhelm
nodes.description: Nasal bark at 600-4,000 Hz. Highest-pitched goose. Simple individual call amplified by 10,000-50,000+ birds into continuous dissonant roar. Mass liftoffs physically immersive. Audible for a mile+. One of the most overwhelming avian sounds in the PNW.

nodes.title: Blue Morph -- Single-Gene Color Polymorphism
nodes.description: White and blue morphs controlled by a single gene with incomplete dominance. Blue morph once classified as separate species ('Blue Goose'). Species named from the dark form: caerulescens = 'becoming blue.' Blue morphs uncommon in Skagit Valley flocks (eastern Arctic origin).

nodes.title: Conservation Irony -- Too Many Geese
nodes.description: Saved from overhunting. Population exploded to 15M+ from agricultural food subsidies. Now overgrazing Arctic tundra breeding habitat -- 'eat-outs' destroying vegetation for decades. Light Goose Conservation Order (1999). Conservation success became ecological crisis.

nodes.title: Skagit Valley Spectacle -- White Blizzards
nodes.description: Fir Island and surrounding Skagit Valley agricultural fields. 30,000-50,000+ birds at peak. White blankets visible from distance. Mass liftoffs = 'white blizzard.' One of the PNW's great wildlife events. Tourism driver. December-February peak.

nodes.title: Goose Quintet Position 5 (129-133)
nodes.description: Canada (129, deep honk, E=5), Cackling (130, higher cackle, E=4), Brant (131, low murmur, E=4), White-fronted (132, musical laugh, E=4), Snow (133, shrill bark, E=5). Five geese, five sounds. One more: Ross's Goose (134, FINAL).

nodes.title: Sixteenth Consecutive Anatidae (118-133)
nodes.description: E=5 breaks the E=4 plateau (130-132). Nearly doubled the Picidae record (9). One more entry (Ross's Goose, 134) before the streak ends at seventeen -- the all-time record by a massive margin.

cross_references.relationship: ANSER PAIR! White-fronted (132, E=4, musical laugh, small-moderate flocks) to Snow (133, E=5, shrill bark, massive flocks). Both Anser genus. Musical vs. dissonant. The contrast within a genus.
E=5 PAIR! Canada (129, E=5, deep honk, large flocks) and Snow (133, E=5, shrill bark, massive flocks). Both achieve E=5 through flock scale. Opposite acoustic character: deep vs. shrill.
COMPARISON PAIR! Snow (133, E=5, large, massive flocks) and Ross's (134, E=4, tiny, mixed in Snow flocks). Mirrors Canada/Cackling (129/130). Ross's = the FINAL Anatidae.
SIXTEEN CONSECUTIVE! Harlequin (118, E=5, stream) to Snow Goose (133, E=5, delta). Sixteen consecutive Anatidae. One more to go.
ENERGY SURGE! Trumpeter (128, E=6 climax). After E=4 plateau (130-132), Snow Goose surges to E=5. Flock scale driving energy back up.
